question.text: Which of the following correctly represents the payoff for a short USDINR futures position?

answer.text: Profit when USDINR falls (INR appreciates) and loss when USDINR rises (INR depreciates): Payoff = Entry Price - Exit Price
Profit when USDINR rises and loss when USDINR falls: Payoff = Exit Price - Entry Price
Fixed profit regardless of USDINR movement equal to the futures premium
No payoff until expiry — profits and losses are only realized on the last trading day

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Short futures profit = Sell price - Buy price = Entry - Exit. If USDINR falls (say from 83 to 81), the short seller gains Rs. 2 per unit. If USDINR rises (say to 85), the short seller loses Rs. 2 per unit. This is ideal for exporters who will receive USD. **The Trap:** Futures don't have premiums like options. Also, futures are marked-to-market daily — profits and losses are realized daily, not just at expiry. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series I covers short futures for exporters. The same linear payoff structure applies to equity (VIII) and bond (IV) short futures. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Short futures = bearish on the underlying price. For currency: exporter shorts to lock in selling rate. For bonds: holder shorts to protect against rate rises.
